speaker: interviewer
text: It is January 17th, 1997. The survivor is Harry Konar, K-O-N-A-R. We are in Riviera Beach, Florida, USA. And this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with reference to my son Larry, the youngest one, but we've had him under psychiatric care since he was born. And about three, four years ago, at the time when I went to Poland and I came back, the doctors, in one of the sessions that we had-- we used to have weekly or biweekly, twice a month or so sessions with a group of doctors. And there was also a group of doctors, of student doctors, observing the conversation. My wife was in it, and him, at one time, even the whole family was in it-- asked me what was the worst trip-- thing from the trip that I can relay and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time, I thought about it. What can you tell? You've been to so many concentration camps, and you saw so many things. And I said, I think that I walked into a crematorium and saw everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] And I walked out, on the other side, and my family didn't. They couldn't do it. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't realize until you are exposed to it. All that survived were from your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the three of us, and my cousin. And it was a very large family. We were closer to my mother's side, because only my father passed away in '32. And most of-- only my one uncle lived in the same town. The rest of them didn't live in the same town. So things were not-- you didn't just pick up and go just to visit. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your life with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce your wife to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Norma. We're going to be married 43 years in April, and we've been very happy together. We've had ups and downs, family problems, but we overcame. We overcome it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Norma, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Harry's a very patient man, and a good man. And, as he said, we've had a-- a good life. Very thankful. And may we have it for another-- as many years as God wishes us to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce the gentleman here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. To my right, my brother Morris. It's hard offhand to describe what we went through during the war or at home. We were kids. We went to school, we-- like every other kid. Played together, fought together. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? And everything else that goes with it. But we had a happy life. And this is my [POLISH] in Polish, the youngest one. My youngest brother, [LAUGHS] [NON-ENGLISH] Remember? They used to call.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. What is there to say? We-- unfortunately, we had-- didn't have good years when we were growing up. We had the war, and it wiped out a lot of good memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were together a good bit of the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that had something to do with your all surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. One thing I did think now-- whatever I was going to tell you before when the incident happened-- Morris and I worked together at the Wehrmacht with the German army. And Bill got into the munition factory. One day I go to visit him with a soldier, and he is not there. Find out later, he escaped. I was scared, and somebody tell me, you better get out of here fast, because they keep you until he shows up again. So I got out from this camp fast. And then we got in touch with him. He was in a small ghetto at the time. A few days later, I went down with the soldier outside the wall of the ghetto, and escaped from-- he escaped from there. And we bought him to our camp, and we had him there. And he survived there with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the Wehrmacht, where we were together. It was 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they never knew what happened to him over at the munitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never found. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say something about that, Bill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you remember? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Nah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't want to go back that far. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to say about your brother or your other brother here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Harry is, is the oldest and the bravest. What else are we going to say?

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Want me to go? Well, I was 14 when the war broke out in 1939. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Speak up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And obviously we lost-- we were teenagers. We didn't go through the life like the normal teenagers would, but the war wasn't over until 1945. We obviously went through an awful lot. But I'm glad  we're here. I'm glad we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am too. Thank you all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel like I'm in the movies. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture you have there now is my mother's younger sister, and her five kids. This was taken in the early '30s in Maciejowice. Her husband at that time worked for his father in Warsaw, and he used to come home only for weekends, Friday afternoon. And in this picture was taken when he was absent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Goldie and her last name is Mirell, M-I-R-E-L-L. I know the first children-- the oldest children's name, Leibel, Sara, and Moshe. I don't recall the names of the two younger kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This picture you're looking at-- to the-- to the right is our aunt named Raizel. I don't even know how you spell it. And next to her is our older sister. It was taken approximately '35, '36, someplace in there, at-- for a summer vacation they went together in, in Poland. I don't even know where. It could be Garbatka or-- Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your aunt's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt's last name was Sztutwoiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was married to your mother;s--

speaker: interviewee
text: Brother. You spell it S-Z-T-U-T-W-O-I-N-E-R, Sztutwoiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken approximately in 1935. That's my mother's youngest sister. Her husband's name was Mirell. M-I-R-E-L-L. And this was on the-- at this brother's-- youngest brother's wedding. And it's the whole family, plus their own-- two of their own children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- can you name some of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only-- the two grandchildren I can name, which were my cousins, Leibel and Sara. The rest of them, I knew my brother-- my uncle's name was Israel, his first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the bride you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the bride?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your, your-- this-- mother's younger sister is in the middle on the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the back row?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the back row, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name again is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this was in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm. This is a picture of myself, on the right, and my close friend and neighbor Moshe Pshititski. This was taken in March of 1941 in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He and his whole family, which were our neighbors since I can remember, for years-- nobody survived. This passport was taken in 1941 in Radom. In order to leave together or go any place, you had to have identification. You couldn't go any place without identification. This is the inside part of the passport with my picture on it. The picture-- on the right is me, and in the middle is brother Morris and brother William. It was taken in the early part of 1946, before Bill left for America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stuttgart, Germany. This picture's of my wife and I and our two sons, Larry and Charles, Charles's wife, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephen.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of his babies, Stephen. The younger one wasn't born yet. Nina wasn't born yet at the time when this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is Charles?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Charles, [POINTS] right here, with the beard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They both have beards. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the middle one is Larry. Yeah. And this was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Radom in our apartment--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not, not Rochester?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Rochester.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately-- he was born in '84, Stephen, so this would be '85, a year later. OK. This picture was taken in Rochester approximately six, seven years ago. It's our grandson, Stephen, and our granddaughter, Nina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
